coredata.dc:title: Effect of solubilization pH and dehulling on hempseed protein recovery: Chemical characterization, physical properties, and preliminary study of carbon dots synthesis from residual solids

coredata.dc:description: Consumer demand for healthier and more sustainable food products is steadily increasing, with strong interest in alternative protein sources such as hempseed (Cannabis sativa L.). However, the shift toward new protein sources requires more than merely identifying alternatives; it also necessitates optimized processing for high recovery yields and complete raw material utilization. In this study a zero-waste fractionation approach was developed to enhance protein recovery from whole and dehulled hempseeds. Following oil extraction, protein recovery was evaluated under various solubilization pH values (10, 11 and 12). Results showed that the recovery of protein isolates was significantly improved at high solubilization pH. Specifically, the highest protein recovery (22.23 g) and purity (94.5 % Kjeldahl protein) were obtained from dehulled hempseeds at pH 12. Amino acid profiles of protein isolates were similar; however, FTIR analysis revealed differences in the secondary structures of whole and dehulled protein isolates, with dehulled isolates exhibiting lower α-helix and higher β-sheet contents. Similarly, the physical properties of whole and dehulled protein isolates differed, with the latter exhibiting lower solubility and higher oil holding capacity and foaming stability. In addition, to fully utilize hempseeds, a zero-waste approach was developed by producing photoluminescent carbon dots from residual solids. Ultimately, this study is the first to propose an integrated processing concept that not only maximizes hempseed protein recovery yields but also incorporates a sustainable valorization strategy for residual solids, offering a promising approach toward the establishment of sustainable agri-food systems.

head.ce:title: Effect of solubilization pH and dehulling on hempseed protein recovery: Chemical characterization, physical properties, and preliminary study of carbon dots synthesis from residual solids

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Consumer demand for healthier and more sustainable food products is steadily increasing, with strong interest in alternative protein sources such as hempseed (Cannabis sativa L.). However, the shift toward new protein sources requires more than merely identifying alternatives; it also necessitates optimized processing for high recovery yields and complete raw material utilization. In this study a zero-waste fractionation approach was developed to enhance protein recovery from whole and dehulled hempseeds. Following oil extraction, protein recovery was evaluated under various solubilization pH values (10, 11 and 12). Results showed that the recovery of protein isolates was significantly improved at high solubilization pH. Specifically, the highest protein recovery (22.23 g) and purity (94.5 % Kjeldahl protein) were obtained from dehulled hempseeds at pH 12. Amino acid profiles of protein isolates were similar; however, FTIR analysis revealed differences in the secondary structures of whole and dehulled protein isolates, with dehulled isolates exhibiting lower α-helix and higher β-sheet contents. Similarly, the physical properties of whole and dehulled protein isolates differed, with the latter exhibiting lower solubility and higher oil holding capacity and foaming stability. In addition, to fully utilize hempseeds, a zero-waste approach was developed by producing photoluminescent carbon dots from residual solids. Ultimately, this study is the first to propose an integrated processing concept that not only maximizes hempseed protein recovery yields but also incorporates a sustainable valorization strategy for residual solids, offering a promising approach toward the establishment of sustainable agri-food systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The growing need to establish a sustainable agri-food system has been emphasized through various strategies, such as the Circular Economy Action Plan, the Farm to Fork Strategy, and the 17 Sustainable Development Goals (established by the United Nations), which aim to expedite our shift towards a sustainable food system. In this context, industrial hemp (Cannabis sativa L.) could be used as a sustainable and eco-friendly source of nutritious food products, as hemp plants require less water and fewer pesticides, compared to most other crops (Yano and Fu, 2023).

Hemp or industrial hemp, one of the oldest cultivated crops, is a non-drug Cannabis used for a variety of purposes, such as food, cosmetic and fabric production (Cravotto et al., 2024; Leonard et al., 2020). It is worth noting that the industrial hemp global market was valued at USD 6.8 billion in 2022 and it is expected to reach USD 18.1 billion by 2027 (Anonymous, 2022). Until now, hemp cultivars are intended to be used mainly for fibers (hemp bast and hurd) and, to a lesser extent, for seeds production (Anonymous, 2022; Brkljača et al., 2023).

Given the increasing consumer demand for plant-based food products and the desire for healthier, environmentally sustainable diets, hempseed is attracting attention for its excellent nutritional value (Farinon et al., 2020). More specifically, hempseed is a high-quality and nutrient-rich food, consisting mainly of 25–35 % oil (rich in PUFAs), 20–25 % protein (rich in essential amino acids), 20–30 % carbohydrates, 10–15 % insoluble fibers, vitamins and useful minerals, such as phosphorous, potassium, magnesium, sodium, calcium, iron, copper and zinc (Wang and Xiong, 2019; Leonard et al., 2020; Subratti et al., 2019).

Currently, hempseed is predominantly utilized for oil extraction, while the resultant hempseed meal, arising as a by-product of this process, serves as animal feedstock and as dietary supplement Moreover, hempseed protein is gaining attention for the development of innovative food applications, including edible film packaging solutions (Dash et al., 2024). Hempseed meal contains a substantial amount of protein (around 50 %) with a balanced amino acid profile (Leonard et al., 2020; Wang and Xiong, 2019), however its content in antinutritional compounds, such as phytic acid, glucosinolates, and condensed tannins, could impede the absorption of not only minerals but also protein (Leonard et al., 2020). Apart from antinutrient content, protein properties such as functionality and digestibility are influenced by the dehulling process and protein extraction methods (Shen et al., 2020a).

While numerous studies have evaluated the effects of dehulling and extraction methods on hemp protein quality (Alonso-Esteban et al., 2022; Dong et al., 2024; Shen et al., 2020a), there is a lack of research specifically focused on methods to achieve high recovery yields and purity of hemp protein. As a result, the full potential of industrial hempseed remains underutilized (Burton et al., 2022). Therefore, this study focused on the effect of solubilization pH to maximize protein recovery, addressing the limited research on efficient protein extraction. Moreover, the physical properties of the extracted proteins were studied to investigate the impact of processing on their suitability for food applications. This study also presents the first attempt to fully exploit hempseeds through a zero-waste processing approach, incorporating the development of a hydrothermal process to valorize hempseed solid residues for the synthesis of photoluminescent carbon dots, offering an integrated and promising pathway toward the establishment of sustainable agri-food systems.

Hempseeds (Finola variety) were supplied by the company Hempture (Dublin, Ireland) and were used as the source for protein, oil and phenolic extraction in this study. All the chemicals used were of analytical grade.

The crude ash, lipid, moisture and protein (%N × 6.25) contents for the resulting isolate was determined according to the Association of Official Analytical Chemists (AOAC, 2003) methods 923.03, 920.85, 925.10, and 920.87, respectively. The carbohydrate content was calculated as the percentage difference from 100 %.

Hempseed meal was obtained from whole and dehulled hempseeds following a modified protocol described by Setti et al. (2020). In brief, hempseeds were milled with an electric grinder and dried at 50 °C for 12 h. The dried samples were defatted using three successive hexane extractions (1:3 hempseeds:hexane ratio, w/v), each lasting 2 h. The defatted samples were then dried at 50 °C until solvent evaporation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery of hemp protein isolate (HPI) through isoelectric precipitation
ce:section.ce:para:
Hemp protein isolates were prepared from the dried, defatted samples using the alkaline isoelectric precipitation technique, as described by Hadnađev et al. (2018), with some modifications. Briefly, the defatted whole and dehulled hempseed meal were dissolved in water at 1:20 ratio (w/v) and the pH was adjusted to 10, 11 and 12 with 1 M NaOH under constant stirring for 2 h at 35 °C. After centrifugation at 4637×g for 20 min at 4 °C (Rotina 420R, Hettich Zentrifugen), the resulting supernatant was collected, and the residual solids were oven-dried, until reaching a constant weight to determine their dry weight. The supernatant was used to recover the crude protein fraction by adjusting the pH to 5 with 1 M HCl and leaving the suspension to stand overnight at 4 °C. The crude protein precipitate was collected by centrifugation (4637×g, 20 min, 4 °C) and was washed with water three times to remove salts, whereas the supernatant (residual liquid stream or hemp milk) was oven-dried, until constant weight, to determine its dry matter. The protein precipitate was collected and dissolved in water and the pH was adjusted to 7.0 with NaOH 1 M, then freeze-dried (BK-FD12P, Biobase Biodustry Co Ltd., Jinan, China) for 48 h. The effect of the solubilization pH on the recovery of protein was evaluated by determining the protein purity using the Kjeldahl method (as described in section 2.2). Additionally, extraction and crude protein yields were calculated as follows:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of phenolic compounds from hempseed hulls
ce:section.ce:para: The hulls obtained after the dehulling of hempseeds were further used to extract phenolic compounds (Kachrimanidou et al., 2015). Specifically, a precisely weighed sample was mixed with 40 % (v/v) ethanol and then ultrasonicated (Sonica 2400 M S3, Soltec, Milano, Italy) for 20 min. After centrifugation (4637×g, 10 °C, 20 min), the extracts were filtrated and evaporated using a vacuum rotary evaporator (Rotary IKA, RV 10 digital V) at 40 °C. The process was repeated two times for maximum phenolic compounds recovery. The obtained extracts were dissolved in 5 mL methanol and stored at −20 °C in the dark until characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fatty acid composition of hempseed oil
ce:section.ce:para: The fatty acid methyl esters (FAMEs) were prepared by trans-esterification of hempseed oil with 2 N KOH in methanol and n-hexane. FAMEs were analyzed by GC using a Shimadzu GC-2010 Pro equipped with FID detector and a MEGA-10 column (30 m × 0.25 mm i.d., 0.25 μm film thickness). Helium was used as carrier gas (3 mL/min). The oven temperature was programmed at 60 °C for 1 min and then increased to 245 °C, at a rate of 8 °C/min, and maintained for 5 min. The temperature of the injector and the detector were set at 245 °C. The split ratio was 1:100 and the sample injection volume was 1 μL. Peak identification was accomplished by comparison of retention times with those of a certified reference FAME mixture (Supelco 37 Component FAME mix). Fatty acid data were expressed as area percentage of FAME.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of total phenolic content and antioxidant activity index
ce:section.ce:para:
The total phenolic content (TPC) of the extracts was estimated spectrophotometrically (Helios Omega, ThermoScientific, USA) at 750 nm by the Folin-Ciocalteu colorimetric method according to the methods described in our previous publication (Kachrimanidou et al., 2015) and the results were quantified using a gallic acid standard curve and were expressed as mg gallic acid equivalents (GAE)/g of dry hull. The antioxidant activity of the extracts (5.8 mg/L) was estimated using DPPH• (2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl) radical scavenging assay (Tsouko et al., 2020). The results were expressed as antioxidant activity as follows:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of phenolic compounds by HPLC
ce:section.ce:para: Analysis of individual phenolic compounds was carried out using an Agilent 1200 series apparatus coupled to a diode array detector. The separation was achieved by an Agilent Eclipse XDB-C18 column (4.6 × 150 mm, 5 μm) at ambient temperature. The analytical parameters were based on the method reported by Alexandri et al. (2016) with some modifications. More specifically, 0.1 % aqueous solution of perchloric acid (Solvent A) and MeOH (Solvent B) were used as mobile phase with 1 mL/min flow rate at ambient temperature, with an injection volume of 20 μL. The gradient elution program used was as follows: 100 % A 0–5 min, 0–5 min 100 % solvent A, at 25 min 75 % A and 25 % B, at 45 min 60 % A and 40 % B, at 60 min 100 % B, at 70.01 min 100 % A and finally 70.01–75 min 100 % A. Chromatograms were monitored at 280, 325 and 360 nm and peak identification was based on retention times and on-line spectra data related to the standard compounds. Quantification was carried out based on calibration curves for each compound using standard solutions.

The FTIR analysis of HPI was carried out with an Agilent Cary 630 FTIR Spectrometer equipped with ATR sampling module. The IR spectrum was recorded by measuring the absorbance in the range of 650–4000 cm−1 with 4 cm−1 resolution. Background and sample measurements were collected after 32 scans each.

The determination of the secondary structure of hemp protein was based on the deconvolution of the Amide I peak (1600-1700 cm−1). Initially the baseline was subtracted and rescaled and the peak selection was based on the secondary derivative of the absorption. For the smoothing of the secondary derivative the Savitzky-Golay method was applied and peak fitting was carried out using Gaussian fitting. Peak assignment was based on literature sited results and expressed as percentage according to the peak area.

Initially, protein hydrolysis was performed according to Shen et al. (2020a). More specifically, 50 mg of whole and dehulled protein isolates were added into 10 mL 6 N HCl containing 0.15 mL of phenol and stored in 80 °C for 30 min. Then, the solutions were transferred at 105 °C for 24 h. After hydrolysis, samples were cooled down and diluted to 50 mL with distilled water. Then, 2 mL of the hydrolyzed samples were filtered with a 0.2 μm polytetrafluoroethylene filter and freeze dried. Then, 2 mL citric acid buffer was added to the freeze-dried samples and vortexed for 10 s.

The reagents for amino acids derivatization were Ortho-Phthalaldehyde (OPA) and Fluorenylmethoxy chloroformate (FMOC). For the preparation of OPA reagent, 10 mg of OPA were diluted in 1 mL borate buffer 0.4 M (pH 10.2) and 23 μL of 3-mercaptopropionic acid (up to 2.8 % w/v final concentration) were added. For the FMOC reagent, 2.5 mg of FMOC were diluted in 1 mL acetonitrile. Derivatization reaction was carried out in an Agilent G1329A autosampler using injection program according to Agilent 5990-4547 EN Application Note (Henderson and Brooks, 2010). Both derivatization reagents were freshly prepared prior to analysis and stored at 4 °C.

Amino acid analysis of the whole and dehulled hemp protein was carried out based on the method proposed by Agilent Technologies and reported on 5990-4547 EN Application Note (Henderson and Brooks, 2010), with few deviations. More specifically, an Agilent 1200 series HPLC equipped with a diode array detector (DAD) and a Zorbax Eclipse Plus C18 column (4.6 × 150 mm, 5 μm) were used. Mobile phase was consisted of 10 mM Na2HPO4: 10 mM Na2B4O7, pH 8.2: 5 mM NaN3 (Solvent A) and acetonitrile: methanol: water (45:45:10, v: v: v) (Solvent B) and the flow rate was 1 mL/min. The gradient elution program was as follows: 0–0.84 min 98 % Solvent A and 2 % Solvent B, at 33.4 min 43 % A and 57 % B, at 33.5 min 100 % Solvent B, 33.5–39.3 min 100 % B, at 39.4 min 98 % A and 2 % B, 39.4–60 min 98 % A and 2 % B. The temperature of the analysis was kept constant at 40 °C and the chromatograms were monitored at 262 and 338 nm.

The protein solubility of HPI at different pH was determined according to the method described by Tomotake et al. (2002) with slight modifications. Briefly, aqueous protein solutions were prepared (1 % w/v) and their pH was adjusted to different pH ranging from 2.0 to 12.0 with 1 M HCl or 1 M NaOH. The solutions were stirred at room temperature for 1 h and centrifuged (4637×g, 30 min). The supernatant was filtered and the protein content was determined by the Lowry method (Lowry et al., 1951). Additionally, for the determination of total protein content (as the control), the protein sample was dissolved in 0.1 M NaOH. The percentage of protein solubility (PS) was then calculated using the following equation:

Surface hydrophobicity of HPI was determined using the hydrophobic chromophore bromophenol blue (BPB), which bounds on insoluble proteins, following the method described by Feng et al. (2015), with some modifications. Briefly, 200 μL of BPB solution (1 mg/mL) was added to 1 mL of HPI solution (5 mg/mL) prepared in 0.02 M phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) at pH 6. Following a 10-min shaken incubation in the dark, the protein solution was centrifuged (4637×g, 10 min, 4 °C). Then, the free BPB in the supernatant was diluted (1:10, v/v) in PBS solution and optical density (OD) was measured at 595 nm. The control sample underwent the same procedure but without containing HPI. The index of hydrophobicity was calculated as follows:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water and oil holding capacity (WHC and OHC)
ce:section.ce:para:
The WHC and OHC were determined according to a modified method of Lin et al. (2021). In brief, 20 mL of deionized water or soybean oil were added to 0.2 g dried protein. The dispersions were vortexed for 30 s and then centrifuged at 4637×g for 20 min at 10 °C. After centrifugation, the supernatant was decanted and the sediment was weighed. The WHC and OHC (g of water or oil per g of protein) were calculated as follows:

)

W 0
W 0
where W0 is the weight of the dry sample (g) and W1 is the net weight of the sediment (g).

The foaming capacity (FC) and stability (FS) were determined following the method of (Yin et al., 2008). Briefly, 0.1 g of protein was dispersed in 10 mL PBS (pH 7.0) and the volume was measured. Then, the solution was homogenized using a high-speed homogenizer (HG-15D, Witeg Labortechnik GMBH, Wertheim, Germany) at 15000 rpm for 3 min and the total volume was measured. The volume was measured again after standing for 30 min and FC and FS were calculated using the following equations:

where V0 is the volume of solution (mL), V1 is the volume of solution after homogenization (mL) and V2 is the volume of solution after standing for 30 min of (mL).

Surface tension measurement was carried out according to Fang et al. (2023) with some modifications. Specifically, the surface tension of HPI solutions was performed by the Wilhelmy plate method, at 25 °C, using a tensiometer (K20 Krüss tensiometer, KRÜSS GmbH, Germany) equipped with a 1.9-cm platinum plate (wetted length: 40.20 mm). HPI solution (1 % w/v) was adjusted to pH 3.0, 5.0, 7.0 and 9.0 and stirred at 800 rpm for 2 h. Then, the aqueous protein dispersion was centrifuged at 3500 rpm for 1 min to remove the insoluble aggregates. The tensiometer was calibrated with distilled water. All measurements were conducted in triplicate and the results represent the mean values.

An amount of 0.5 g of solid material was added in 15 mL water and placed in a 30-mL teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The autoclave was maintained at 180 °C for 12 h and then left to cool down slowly to room temperature. The resulting solution was first filtered through a plain filter paper, then filtered again through a 0.2 μm hydrophilic filter and finally centrifuged at 15000 g for 30 min. The supernatant was diluted with water and observed under ultraviolet light (254 nm). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the aqueous suspension of hempseed carbon dots was obtained using the Nanophotometer (Implen, Germany). Moreover, the fluorescence excitation spectrum (at λem = 460 nm) and the emission spectrum (at λex = 380 nm) of carbon dot suspensions in water was determined with the PerkinElmer LS-50B spectrofluorometer (PerkinElmer, UK).

Statistical analysis tests were employed to assess significant differences at a level of 5 % (P < 0.05) among treatments using analysis of variance (ANOVA) and Tukey's HSD (honest significant difference). These analyses were conducted using Microsoft Excel software (version 2016). The reported values represent the mean values ± standard version (SD) derived from three independent experiments or analyses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fractionation and mass balance of whole hempseed
ce:section.ce:para: The proximate analysis of whole hempseed revealed a moisture content of 3.34 ± 0.44 % (w/w), and constituted of (w/w, on dry basis): oil 33.03 ± 0.21 %, protein 22.94 ± 0.37 %, total carbohydrates 35.39 ± 0.61 % and ash 5.31 ± 0.04 %. The results of the current study are in agreement with other studies performed for the Finola hempseed variety. Specifically, Callaway (2004) reported that the whole hempseed of the Finola variety contained 35.5 % (w/w) oil and 24.8 % (w/w) protein. It is worth noting that the composition of hempseeds varies among different cultivars, geographical regions, climatic conditions, and local agronomic factors (House et al., 2010). In particular, the hempseeds of Finola variety have been reported to contain 29.3–31.3 % (w/w) oil and 21.9–23.8 % (w/w) protein. Compared to other varieties, Finola contained the highest amount of oil and protein, whereas the lowest oil and protein level were reported for Fedora and Anka varieties, respectively (Siano et al., 2018; Vonapartis et al., 2015).

The whole hempseeds fractionation targeted to the recovery of high-nutritional and functional compounds, such as oil, protein of high purity and phenolic compounds-rich extracts. The schematic representation of whole hempseed fractionation is depicted in Fig. 1 . The solvent extraction, led to the recovery of 96.06 % of oil, resulting in hempseed meal of 68.27 ± 0.38 g (d.b.). Then, isoelectric precipitation, which is the most used procedure to prepare protein isolate from oilseeds, was employed to obtain the protein fraction from hempseed meal. As one can observe in Fig. 1, the amount of precipitated protein as well as the purity were strongly dependent on the solubilization pH. Results showed that the mass of precipitated crude protein was low (4.76 ± 0.23 g, d.b.) at pH 10, and it gradually increased to 17.88 ± 0.49 g (d.b.) as the pH of the solution increased to 12. The use of solubilization pH values far from the isoelectric point enhanced protein solubility, thus resulting in increased recovery of crude protein. Notably, as the solubilization pH decreased, the obtained mass of the residual solids fraction increased, indicating that a significant amount of protein was discarded along with the residual solids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fractionation and mass balance of dehulled hempseed
ce:section.ce:para: Dehulled hempseed had a moisture content of 2.43 ± 0.01 % (w/w) and contained also (w/w, on d.b.): oil 50.36 ± 0.91 %, protein 30.91 ± 0.51 %, total carbohydrates 9.64 ± 1.04 % and ash 6.66 ± 0.06 %. As it was expected, dehulled hempseeds exhibited higher oil and protein contents, due to the absence of hulls which accounted for 42 % (w/w) of the whole hempseed. Regarding the oil content, our results are consistent with those of Zając et al. (2019) who reported an oil content of 51.17 % (w/w) for the Białobrzeskie hemp strain. Also, Alonso-Esteban et al. (2022) reported that eight commercial samples of dehulled hemp seeds had an average protein content of 26 % (w/w). A similar level of protein (33 %) with our study was reported by Callaway & Pate (2009) for the Finola cultivar.

Fig. 2 presents the processing of 100 g (d.b.) dehulled hempseeds. As in the case of whole hempseed, nearly all of the oil was recovered (94.60 %), leaving 52.36 ± 0.28 g (d.b.) of hemp meal. Subsequently, isoelectric precipitation of protein resulted in higher recovery of crude protein at solubilization pH 12, than at pH 10 and 11. In particular, 22.23 ± 0.47 g of crude protein was precipitated at pH 12. On the other hand, the lowest mass of precipitated protein (8.75 ± 0.38 g) was observed when the solubilization pH was adjusted at 10. These results indicated that the solubilization pH had significant impact on the recovery yields of protein, which is further evaluated in a following section (3.3.2). Furthermore, the results demonstrated that using dehulled hempseeds led to the recovery of higher amount of crude protein, compared to the mass balance results of whole hempseed (Fig. 1). Clearly, the direct exposure of hemp hearts to the solubilization solution enhanced protein solubility, resulting in higher crude protein recovery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fatty acid composition of hempseed oil
ce:section.ce:para: The main unsaturated fatty acids of hempseed oil were linoleic acid (54.82 %), α-linolenic acid (18.74 %) and oleic acid (11.12 %). The polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFAs) in hemp oil were 78.03 % of the total fatty acids, while the monounsaturated fatty acids accounted for 11.12 % (Table S1). Hemp oil exhibited a high PUFA/Saturated ratio of 9.44, which has been reported to be beneficial to health (Chen and Liu, 2020). Its high PUFAs concentration also aligns with the reference intake recommendations set by the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA, 2009). Similarly, the ω-6 to ω-3 ratio was 3.16, consistent with previous findings (Petrović et al., 2015), and is regarded as optimal for human health (Callaway, 2004).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery yields and purity of crude protein fractions
ce:section.ce:para: The extraction yields through isoelectric precipitation at different solubilization pH values (10, 11 and 12), along with their respective protein purity and recovered mass are presented in Table 1 . The extraction and the crude protein yield were significantly increased (p < 0.05) as the solubilization pH increased from 10 to 12, for both whole and dehulled hempseeds, indicating improved protein solubility under more alkaline conditions. Actually, at high alkaline pH, extracted proteins acquire a negative charge, resulting in electrostatic repulsion that maintains protein dispersion. This repulsion enhances protein-solvent interactions, thereby enhancing protein solubility (Valenzuela et al., 2013). More specifically, crude protein yields increased approximately 3-fold at pH 12, compared to pH 10. Similar findings were reported for yellow pea protein, which showed 17 % increase in protein yield, after shifting the pH from 8.5 to 9.5 (Gao et al., 2020). Additionally, other researchers reported that protein yields of pigeonpea and cowpea increased as the solubilization pH increased from 8.5 to 12.

The results of protein content indicated that high purity protein fractions were collected in all solubilization pH values, regardless the presence of hulls (Table 1). It is also noteworthy that protein purity was significantly increased when the solubilization pH was adjusted from 10 to 11 (p < 0.05). However, the protein purity was not significantly changed when solubilization pH was above 11 (p > 0.05). Τhe protein purity determined at solubilization pH 10 was similar to the value (82.23 %) reported by Shen et al. (2020a), who also used the isoelectric precipitation procedure to recover protein from whole hempseeds.

Statistical analysis of whole and dehulled hempseeds revealed that the presence of hulls significantly influenced the protein extraction yields, as demonstrated in Table 1. In particular, the dehulling process increased both the extraction and crude protein yields, which could be related to the removal of phenolic compounds and fibers of the hulls. As previously reported, interactions between protein and phenolic compounds or fibers may negatively affect protein solubility, thereby reducing the extraction yield and protein content of whole hempseed (Shen et al., 2020a). Overall, the results of protein purity indicated that the differences above pH 10 were statistically insignificant (p > 0.05), for both whole and dehulled hempseeds, and the crude protein fractions obtained at pH 11 and 12 can be characterized as protein isolates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of phenolic compounds-rich fraction and characterization
ce:section.ce:para: Phenolic compounds are among the most widespread bioactive substances found in food and agri-food by-products (Messinese et al., 2024). In hempseed, the TPC differs from one fraction to another. Most research has focused on the extraction of hemp oil and protein, while studies aimed at the extraction and characterization of phenolic compounds from hempseeds are scarce. In our study, the hull fraction contained 1.15 ± 0.09 mg GAE/g, with its extracts showing 82.7 ± 5.2 % antioxidant activity. Leonard et al. (2020), reported that TPC of hulls was 2.85 ± 0.05 mg GAE/g, however this discrepancy may due to the different hemp variety and extraction method, as they used 80 % (v/v) methanol for 2 h under nitrogen gas. Moreover, the TPC in the hull fraction was found to be higher compared to the TPC of hempseed meals (0.53 ± 0.02 and 0.36 ± 0.08 mg GAE/g for whole and dehulled hemp meals, respectively), suggesting that the hulls are the main source of phenolic compounds. In accordance with this, previous studies also report that the majority of phenolic compounds are found in the hulls of hempseeds (Chen et al., 2012; Leonard et al., 2021; Siano et al., 2018).

The phenolic compound-rich extract of hempseed hulls was analyzed using HPLC. N-trans-caffeoyl-tytramine, belonging to the group of hydroxycinnamic acid, was the most abundant phenolic compound, accounting for 76.13 ppm (82.17 % of the total identified phenolic compounds). Other hydroxycinnamic acids, such as ferulic and p-coumaric acids (3.34 ppm and 0.16 ppm, respectively) and hydroxybenzoic acids, such as 3,4 dihydroxybenzoic and 4-hydroxybenzoic acids (9.26 ppm and 3.76 ppm, respectively) were also detected to a lesser extent. In general, the polyphenol groups found in hempseed fractions are primarily lignanamides, hydroxycinnamic acid, hydroxybenzoic acid and flavonoids (Leonard et al., 2020). However, consistent with this study, the major phenylpropanoid amide present in hempseed hull is N-trans-caffeoyltyramine (Leonard et al., 2020).

In general, harsh extraction conditions may provide high recovery yields; however, they may also lead to a high denaturation index, thereby affecting the functionality and properties of proteins (Meganaharshini et al., 2023). Therefore, characterizing the physical properties of protein isolates is crucial to assess not only the extraction process, but also to demonstrate possible applications. Consequently, the crude protein fractions that demonstrated high protein yields and purity (pH 12) were further characterized in terms of their chemical and physical properties.

The protein isolates of whole and dehulled hempseeds (whole and dehulled HPI) exhibited similar spectrophotometric results as demonstrated by FTIR analysis (Fig. 3 ). In both cases the strong peak at 3269.90 cm−1 and the weak peak at 3056.42 cm−1 are attributed to the NH stretching vibrations and corresponds to the bands Amide A and Amide B (Barth, 2007). The most characteristic bands of the peptide bond are Amide I at 1600-1700 cm−1 and Amide II at 1480-1580 cm−1. The Amide I band primarily corresponds to CO stretching vibration, while the Amide II band results from the combination of NH bending and CN stretching vibrations (Barth, 2007; Srour et al., 2017). In our IR spectra, the peak at 1628.77 cm−1 is attributed to Amide I, while the peak at 1513.08 cm−1 is attributed to Amide II. Another characteristic peak of proteins is Amide III, which occurs in the range of 1200–1400 cm−1 and is also present in both spectra around 1387 cm−1 (Fig. 3).

As shown in Table 3 , whole and dehulled HPI were characterized with high amount of l-Glutamic acid, l-Aspartic acid and l-Arginine, which is in agreement with previous studies (Hadnađev et al., 2018; Lin et al., 2021; Shen et al., 2020b). The main amino acids in plant proteins are glutamic and aspartic acid, which results in their acidic isoelectric point (Feyzi et al., 2018). Comparing the amino acid composition of whole and dehulled HPI, similar amino acid profiles were observed. Moreover, the ratio of arginine to lysine (Arg:Lys) in the current study was 3.24 in both whole and dehulled HPI, which is higher than that of animal-based proteins, such as casein, and plant proteins, such as soybean protein isolates (Tang et al., 2006). Notably, a high Arg:Lys ratio has been linked to cardioprotective effect, as it is positively associated with hypocholesterolemic and atherogenic effects (Malomo and Aluko, 2015; Shen et al., 2020b).

Solubility is a fundamental property of proteins, as it plays a critical role in food processing conditions and sensory attributes, ultimately determining their potential application in food systems (Grossmann and McClements, 2023; Małecki et al., 2021). Moreover, solubility is an index of hydrophilicity and provides information about the ability of HPI to formulate colloidal dispersions (Chen et al., 2023). The solubility of whole and dehulled HPI as a function of pH is illustrated in Fig. 5 . HPI displayed a typical U-shaped solubility profile, showing a minimum solubility at pH 5, which was 5.56 % and 4.13 % in whole and dehulled HPI, respectively. The solubility of whole HPI was less than 15 %, near to its isoelectric point (pH range of 4–7), while solubility at pH values outside this region showed a gradual increase. Solubility exceeded 90 % above pH 10, reaching a maximum of 95.1 % at pH 12. In the case of dehulled HPI, a similar solubility pattern was noticed. In particular, a solubility of 25 % was observed between pH 3 and 10, whereas the highest solubility of 86.2 % occurred at pH 12. Previous studies have reported similar findings to those of this work, regarding the U-shaped pattern of hempseed protein solubility. However, considerable differences in solubility levels can be observed across studies, which are related to factors such as the pretreatment of hempseed meal (e.g. ball milling), the protein extraction method (e.g. isoelectric point, micellization), the presence of hulls in the starting material (e.g. whole or dehulled hempseeds), and the pH values during solubilization and precipitation (Hadnađev et al., 2018; Julakanti et al., 2023; Shen et al., 2020a, 2020b). To date, the majority of research has focused on protein extraction at solubilization/precipitation pH values of 10/5 or 10/6. In these cases, the solubility of the resulting HPI ranged from 70 % to 90 % at pH 10 (Fang et al., 2023; Hadnađev et al., 2018; Shen et al., 2020a). Consistent with these studies, our findings showed similar results; the whole HPI exhibited 90 % solubility at pH 10, despite using a higher solubilization pH. Studies employing protein extraction at higher solubilization pH levels are limited, resulting in a lack of evidence on solubility behavior of these proteins.

Comparing the whole and dehulled HPI, it was observed that whole HPI demonstrated higher solubility at pH values away from the isoelectric region. This might be due the lower β-sheet content of whole HPI, as revealed by FTIR analysis (section 3.4.1). More specifically, the low solubility of plant proteins has been correlated with high β-sheet content (Fang et al., 2023).

The surface hydrophobicity and surface tension were determined for both whole and dehulled HPI and the results are depicted in Fig. 6 . The surface hydrophobicity was significantly higher at pH 3.0 (p < 0.05) compared to the other pH values, for both whole and dehulled HPI. The lower surface hydrophobicity was observed at pH 5, which is also the isoelectric point of HPI. In this state, insufficient electrostatic repulsion promoted protein self-aggregation with the hydrophobic regions concealed within the aggregates, making them less accessible to BPB during analysis. As a matter of fact, surface hydrophobicity is related to the presence of hydrophobic groups on the surface of protein molecules (Mei et al., 2023). Under acidic conditions, protein denaturation likely occurs, leading to the exposure of hydrophobic regions and, consequently, to increased surface hydrophobicity (Mishyna et al., 2019).

The comparison of whole and dehulled HPI in terms of surface hydrophobicity revealed that whole HPI had more exposed hydrophobic groups on its surface. In particular, surface hydrophobicity of whole HPI was significantly higher than dehulled HPI at pH above 5 (p < 0.05). Considering that surface hydrophobicity serves also as an indicator of protein structural changes (Mei et al., 2023), the results can be attributed to the secondary structure obtained by FTIR analysis. Previous research on soybean protein indicated the proportional correlation between surface hydrophobicity and the α-helix content (Wang et al., 2014). According to these results, heat treatment of the soybean protein led to a decrease in the β-sheet conformation and an increase in the α-helix, along with a simultaneous raise in surface hydrophobicity. This correlation between the secondary structure and surface hydrophobicity may explain the differences between whole and dehulled HPI in our case. In particular, whole HPI exhibited higher surface hydrophobicity compared to dehulled HPI (p < 0.05), which might be attributed to its higher α-helix content.

Furthermore, a proportional correlation appears to exist between the surface hydrophobicity and solubility of HPI, as indicated by zero-order fitting model (R2 = 0.996), demonstrating that surface hydrophobicity increases in parallel with solubility (Figs. 5 and 6). Although this may seem contradictory, previous research has shown that solubility is not solely determined by the exposure of hydrophobic groups, but it is also strongly influenced by the processing conditions used for protein isolation (Malik et al., 2017; Wagner et al., 2000).

Surface tension was similar for both whole and dehulled HPI solutions. In particular, surface tension of whole HPI ranged from 40.87 ± 0.16 to 44.89 ± 0.15 mN/m, and from 42.77 ± 0.21 to 46.24 ± 0.17 mN/m for the dehulled HPI. Xiao et al. (2020) denoted that there is a significant correlation between hydrophobicity and surface tension. Especially, once protein absorption reaches the saturation point any changes in surface tension are then governed only by the hydrophobic properties of the protein. Hence, lower surface tension is linked to higher surface hydrophobicity (Xiao et al., 2020). These results are consistent with this study, as HPI presented high surface hydrophobicity and low surface tension values.

The WHC, OHC and foaming properties were determined for the whole and dehulled HPI and the results are shown in Table 4 . The WHC plays an important role in the development of food products, as it reflects the ability of proteins to interact with water, forming a protein matrix able to entrap water molecules (Chen et al., 2023). In the present study, significantly different WHC were observed between whole and dehulled HPI (p < 0.05). The WHC of protein isolate in the food system depends on its ability to bind water, which is affected by several parameters, such as its solubility, pH, and ionic strength (Lin et al., 2021). The OHC of proteins is important for taste conformation, as it also decreases the development of oxidative staling, increasing the stability throughout the time of storage (Palatnik et al., 2015). Also, OHC indicates the ability of protein molecules to interact with non-polar groups, allowing them to interact with fats (Julakanti et al., 2023). The OHC was significantly higher (p < 0.05) in dehulled HPI (7.86 ± 0.22 g oil/g protein) than the OHC of whole HPI. Ιt has been reported that oil-protein interactions are positively affected by increased surface hydrophobicity, while water absorption in negatively affected (Karabulut and Yemiş, 2022; Malik et al., 2017; Resendiz-Vazquez et al., 2017). Additionally, it has been observed that the extraction method, such as alkaline-extraction and micellization, alters the OHC of HPI as a function of the denaturation degree of the protein (Hadnađev et al., 2018).

Foaming capacity is considered a significant aspect of protein application in food systems, as it determines the adsorption of protein at the interface and the coating of air cells (Chen et al., 2023). The foaming capacity of the whole and dehulled HPI was similar (p > 0.05) (Table 4). Furthermore, foaming stability of the dehulled HPI was significantly higher than foaming stability of whole HPI. The higher foaming capacity suggests that greater interactions with the aqueous phase enhance the ability of the proteins to encapsulate and retain air (Crini and Lichtfouse, 2020). Additionally, the results of this study was found to be higher than the foaming stability (58.8 ± 0.57 %) of HPI reported by Yin et al. (2008). The higher protein content has been associated to higher foaming stability of protein solutions. This is due to the ability of proteins to form viscoelastic interfacial membranes through protein-protein interactions improving the resistance of air bubbles to destabilization (Crini and Lichtfouse, 2020; Malomo et al., 2014).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preliminary evaluation of photoluminescent carbon dots synthesis from residual solids
ce:section.ce:para: The solid hempseed residues derived from the fractionation process were subjected to hydrothermal treatment targeting to the production of fluorescent carbon dots. In recent years, raw natural sources, such as biomass have been used as abundant nontoxic precursor materials for the synthesis of carbon dots with a variety of properties and applications, such as food or food packaging biosensors for the detection of food additives, vitamins and foodborne pathogens (Christopoulou et al., 2021; Wareing et al., 2021). In this context, we conducted a preliminary study to investigate the potential of utilizing the residual streams, resulting from the above mentioned hempseed fractionation, to produce carbon dots. The macroscopic observation of the hydrothermal-pretreated solution under ultraviolet light indicated the presence of photoluminescent components (Fig. 7 a). In particular, the solution emitted bright cyan fluorescence, suggesting the formation of water-soluble luminescent carbon dots. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the aqueous suspension of carbon dots is presented in Fig. 7b. The absorption is attributed to the transition of the CC bonds (π→π∗) and the transition of CO (n →π∗). The fluorescence excitation spectrum (λem = 460 nm) and emission spectrum (λex = 380 nm) (Fig. 7c) further elucidate the optical properties of the carbon dots, showing an 80 nm Stokes shift, which is characteristic of the energy difference between the excitation and emission states. The fluorescence color is influenced by transitions in the electronic bandgap of conjugated π-domains, surface defect states, local fluorophores, and the presence of element doping. Generally, the fluorescence properties of carbon dots are influenced by the interaction between the carbon core and its surrounding chemical groups (Yan et al., 2019). Photoluminescence is one of the most fascinating features of carbon dots, indicating their potential use in sensor applications. This optical property is highly dependent on the surface properties of the carbon dots, and it can be tailored by regulating process parameters, such as the pH of the solution, reaction duration and temperature (Elugoke et al., 2024; Mansuriya and Altintas, 2021). However, there are several other characterization techniques that should be performed for a comprehensive analysis of the produced photoluminescent solution, which will be the scope of a forthcoming study.

The purpose of this study was to maximize the recovery yield of protein from hempseeds, within the development of an integrated, zero-waste processing scheme. Currently, most studies on hemp protein isolation have primarily focused on low solubilization pH levels (typically below 10). However, these studies do not assess the efficiency of the extraction process, resulting in limited knowledge regarding the optimal conditions for maximizing protein recovery yields. This study demonstrated that higher solubilization pH values led to increased extraction yields and protein purity. Furthermore, dehulling process along with the use of solubilization pH 12 resulted in significantly higher protein isolate recovery. The assessment of physical properties of the HPI, which was obtained at solubilization pH of 12, revealed that dehulled HPI had low solubility and high surface hydrophobicity, OHC, and foaming stability, highlighting its potential for use in food applications, such as emulsions. A more in-depth correlation analysis between the secondary structure of proteins and their physical properties could offer valuable insights into the impact of processing on these characteristics, whereas will further determine their potential applications in food products. Furthermore, this study reports that hydrothermal treatment of the residual solids led to the development of a highly fluorescent solution, attributed to the formation of fluorescent carbon dots. While the comprehensive characterization of these carbon dots will be the focus of a forthcoming study, this work presents a preliminary framework for maximizing the full potential of hempseed. This approach could not only enhance its economic value but also contribute to a zero-waste strategy by utilizing all parts of the seed, thereby reducing by-products and waste from the production process.
